content.text: React / Next.js + Claude with the Vercel AI SDK

so the hook can automatically manage streaming, message state, status, stop, retry, and richer message parts. This pattern is usually the fastest way to ship a production-grade Claude chat UI in Next.js because it removes most of the low-level SSE parsing and stream assembly that you would otherwise write by hand.
The Vercel AI SDK splits responsibilities cleanly:
handles the UI state machine in React,
handles network transport to your route, and
handles provider execution and streaming on the server. With Anthropic, the provider package is
, and the provider documentation shows that you create models with
and use them with AI SDK functions such as
A common Next.js App Router setup looks like this:
The browser sends UI messages to
, the route converts them into model-compatible messages, Anthropic streams Claude output, and the route returns a UI message stream response that
The AI SDK chatbot docs show the basic shape clearly: use
automatically manages streaming, state updates, and message merging for you.
This is the official AI SDK UI pattern: let the server produce a UI message stream, and let
If you build Claude chat manually, you must manage fetch state, partial text accumulation, retries, stream decoding, and special cases like cancellation yourself. The AI SDK docs explain that

already gives you message streaming, managed states, error handling, stop, regenerate, and message mutation helpers in one hook. So instead of thinking “how do I parse Claude’s stream,” you think “how do I design the chat experience on top of structured message parts.”
One of the most important current patterns is to render
string. The AI SDK docs recommend this because

supports text, tool invocation, tool results, reasoning, files, and sources, which makes the UI future-proof for richer Claude interactions. If you render only a single string, your app will be harder to extend when you later add tools, attachments, or reasoning displays.
The chatbot docs define four main status values:
. This is useful because it gives you a built-in UI state machine: show a spinner when
. That means you do not need to invent your own loading flags unless you have a special product requirement.
A polished Claude chat UI with
Disable submit while status is not
Show a stop button while status is
Keep the assistant message visible as it streams instead of replacing the whole conversation.
This aligns closely with how users expect Claude- or ChatGPT-style interfaces to behave.
to abort the in-flight fetch and

to rerun the last assistant response. This is especially valuable for Claude because long, thoughtful answers may take time, and users often want to interrupt or retry without refreshing the page. In production, this should be treated as a first-class UX feature, not an optional nicety.
The AI SDK docs recommend showing a generic error message such as “Something went wrong” rather than leaking raw server details to end users. On the client,
option so you can customize what gets surfaced to the UI. This separation lets you log detailed provider errors privately while keeping the public interface safe and clean.
patterns is passing extra request data with either transport-level configuration or per-request options. The docs show that
can inject custom headers, body fields, and credentials, and
can also take a second argument with request-level headers, body, and metadata. That makes it easy to pass things like
, temperature preferences, or feature flags to the backend without baking them into the message text itself.
That pattern is better than stuffing hidden operational state into the prompt because the backend can interpret it structurally and safely.
For more control, the AI SDK transport layer supports

. This allows you to change the payload shape so the client can send only the latest message plus a chat ID, while the server reloads the full stored history itself. This is a strong production pattern because it reduces request size, centralizes persistence, and makes resume/regenerate logic more reliable.
Server loads the full thread by
Server appends the new user message and calls Claude.
That pattern scales better than constantly shipping the full transcript from the browser on every turn.
The Anthropic provider in the AI SDK supports several Claude-specific options beyond the generic chat flow. These include
for request-level provider metadata. In practice, this means you can keep the
UI pattern stable while tuning Claude behavior server-side through
This is powerful because frontend code stays simple while the backend becomes your control plane for Claude behavior.
model works especially well when Claude uses tools because the message
structure can represent more than plain text. Anthropic’s provider docs state that tool call streaming is enabled by default and can be disabled with
, while the AI SDK UI docs recommend rendering structured parts so tool invocations and results can appear properly in the chat. In other words, if you expect Claude to call functions,
is usually a better abstraction than a homemade
The AI SDK docs note that reasoning parts can be forwarded to the client with
, and on the client those parts appear as
. Anthropic’s provider docs add that some Claude models support adaptive or budget-based thinking, and that with newer Opus variants, visible reasoning may require

to avoid a long pause before output appears. So if you want a “show reasoning” feature in a Claude chat UI, the correct pattern is to enable reasoning server-side and render reasoning parts explicitly client-side.
A high-value production pattern is attaching metadata to assistant messages using
. The docs show that you can stream values like
, model name, and total token usage into
, then read them on the client for observability or UX display. This is much cleaner than maintaining a parallel side channel for message metrics.
This lets your frontend show timestamps, token counts, latency hints, or model badges without extra fetches.
hook is great for live interaction, but persistence still belongs in your app architecture, not in the hook itself. A strong pattern is: let
manage the local streaming experience, use chat IDs to map to stored threads, and persist the final message set after completion or incrementally on important lifecycle events such as
callbacks, which makes it easy to hook analytics, persistence, or side effects into the chat lifecycle.

for React, which reduces re-render frequency during streaming by batching updates, for example every 50 ms. This matters when Claude streams quickly or when your message renderer is expensive, because updating on every chunk can create unnecessary render pressure. In a larger Next.js app, this is a practical performance knob rather than a theoretical feature.
also supports file attachments through either
or explicit file objects. The docs explain that image and text file inputs can be converted into multimodal content parts, and these can then be rendered on the client by inspecting file parts in
. For Claude-powered UX, this is the natural pattern for “ask about this screenshot” or “summarize this uploaded text file.”
Text stream vs UI message stream
The AI SDK supports both text-only streams and richer UI message streams. If you use
, the docs warn that tool calls, usage information, and finish reasons are not available, whereas
preserves richer semantics. For Claude in serious product work, the UI message stream pattern is usually the better default because it leaves room for tools, metadata, files, and reasoning.

+ Anthropic when your main goal is to ship a polished Claude chat or assistant in Next.js quickly and safely. It is especially good when you want built-in streaming, cancellation, retry, structured parts, and an easy upgrade path to tool use or multimodal chat. If you instead need raw Claude SSE event control, highly custom stream multiplexing, or provider-specific low-level behavior, then a manual SDK route may still be preferable.
A strong concise explanation is: “With Next.js App Router, I use
in a client component, configure it with
, then in the route handler I call
. That gives me token streaming, message state, stop/regenerate, structured tool-aware message parts, and Claude-specific tuning via
